techniques.evidence_quote: publicly exposed Postman server with access key credentials stored in the project's variables
The attacker gained access to the customer environment through the use of stolen long-term access keys.

the attacker used the API GetCallerIdentity using multiple access keys and from the same IP. GetCallerIdentity is similar to the bash command whoami and gives the attacker information about where they have landed.
we saw three API calls: (two) list users, and one GetSendQuota
GetSendQuota was an Amazon email service feature that 'provides the sending limits for the Amazon SES account'
UpdateAccountSendingEnabled... 'enables or disables email sending across your entire Amazon SES account in the current AWS region'

attack_chain.description: Attacker discovered publicly exposed Postman server containing AWS long-term access key credentials stored in the project's variables, harvesting credentials for multiple IAM accounts (seven keys total).
attack_chain.evidence_quote: Using patterns observed in the affected IAM account names, we came across a publicly exposed Postman server with access key credentials stored in the project's variables.

attack_chain.description: Attacker authenticated to the AWS environment using the stolen long-term IAM access keys from a Kali Linux host at an anomalous IP address belonging to a non-major hosting provider.
attack_chain.evidence_quote: The attacker gained access to the customer environment through the use of stolen long-term access keys... the user agent in question revealed that it was more specifically aws-cli/1.22.34 Python/3.9.11 Linux/5.15.0-kali3-amd64 botocore/1.27.84.

attack_chain.description: Attacker called GetCallerIdentity across multiple access keys from the same source IP to identify the privileges and identity context associated with each stolen key — analogous to running 'whoami' in a cloud environment.
attack_chain.evidence_quote: the attacker used the API GetCallerIdentity using multiple access keys and from the same IP. GetCallerIdentity is similar to the bash command whoami and gives the attacker information about where they have landed.

attack_chain.description: Attacker executed ListUsers API calls (observed twice) to enumerate IAM accounts within the environment, all of which were denied.
attack_chain.evidence_quote: we saw three API calls: (two) list users, and one GetSendQuota (our threat hunting team will tell us why this is important). All were denied and all came from the same access key, but different user agents.

attack_chain.description: Attacker called GetSendQuota to determine the sending limits on the AWS SES account, probing email infrastructure capability before attempting abuse. ~95% of the compromised key's activity was Amazon SES-related.
attack_chain.evidence_quote: Most of that user's activity was Amazon SES-related (around 95%)... GetSendQuota was an Amazon email service feature that 'provides the sending limits for the Amazon SES account.'

attack_chain.description: Attacker repeatedly called UpdateAccountSendingEnabled to toggle the SES email sending capability on/off across the account, indicating preparation to abuse SES for spam/phishing campaigns (prevented by Expel remediation).
attack_chain.evidence_quote: 'UpdateAccountSendingEnabled,' specifically, seemed interesting, as it was called several times (but not excessively, and it seemed to toggle a useful service on or off). Documentation indicated that it 'enables or disables email sending across your entire Amazon SES account in the current AWS region.'

initial_access_vector: Stolen long-term AWS IAM access keys harvested from a publicly exposed Postman server where credentials were stored in project variables
persistence_mechanism: Seven IAM accounts and their associated long-term access keys were actively used; no additional persistence mechanism (e.g., new key creation, role assumption) was explicitly described beyond leveraging the pre-existing keys
privilege_escalation: No privilege escalation described; attacker operated within the permissions already granted to the stolen IAM accounts
